column3: Agility is a practice that means being able to work well with others, stay flexible, and keep improving how you do things, especially when things change or don’t go as planned. Imagine a sports team that’s always adjusting its strategy during a game based on what’s happening on the field—no one ever becomes a perfect player, but the team gets better at working together and adapting over time. Agility is built on beliefs and behaviors like being open to new ideas, learning from mistakes, and listening to feedback from teammates and customers. You can’t measure Agility by counting finished projects; instead, it’s seen in how you and others feel about your ability to adapt, collaborate, and keep learning. Skills like Scrum Management, Project Management, and Kanban Board Management are tools you use to deliver results, and you can become an expert in them by practicing and applying them to real work. These skills help you create things, and you can measure them by how often you use them and how well you do specific tasks. Both Agility (the practice) and these skills need you to keep using them, or you’ll lose your edge, and both require you to want to get better. In tech jobs, Agility helps teams respond quickly to change, deliver better products, and learn from mistakes, while skills help you get the actual work done—together, they make you more successful in any role.
column4: The practice of Agility is important to cross-functional teamwork in a lot of ways. Agility means being able to adapt quickly, work well with others, and respond to changes without getting stuck. In teams where people have different roles—like Scrum Masters, Product Owners, UX Designers, and Project Managers—Agility helps everyone stay flexible and open-minded. For example, if a project suddenly needs to change direction because of new customer feedback, an agile team can adjust their plans and keep moving forward instead of getting frustrated or stuck. Agility also encourages team members to share their work early, ask for feedback, and learn from mistakes, which helps the team deliver better results faster. By practicing Agility, everyone in the team can work together more smoothly, solve problems quickly, and make sure the final product really meets the needs of users and customers.
column5:
Agility is similar to other practices because it’s all about how you work with others, keep learning, and adjust your actions based on what’s happening around you. Like other practices, Agility isn’t something you ever fully master—it’s a set of beliefs and behaviors you keep building over time, especially by reflecting with others and yourself. What makes Agility stand out is its focus on being flexible (adaptability), working closely with your team (interactions with individuals), listening to customers (customer collaboration), delivering results quickly (delivering working results), and learning from mistakes fast (failing fast). These components are about how you act and think, not just what you can make or do, which is what skills are about. Skills, like Scrum Management or Project Management, are things you can become an expert in and are measured by what you produce, while practices like Agility are measured by how you and others see your behaviors and choices.

What makes Agility unique is that it’s all about responding quickly to change, working together, and learning as you go. Unlike other practices that might focus more on planning or following set rules, Agility is about being open to new ideas, adjusting your path when things don’t go as planned, and making sure you’re always improving by getting feedback from both your team and your customers. It’s not just about getting things done, but about how you get things done—by staying flexible, working with others, and not being afraid to fail and try again. This makes Agility especially important in fast-changing environments where you need to adapt and grow all the time.
column6:
This practice is carried out through a set of beliefs and behaviors that help you and your team handle change, work together, and keep improving. Agility is not just about following a process—it’s about being open to new ideas, adjusting your plans when things don’t go as expected, delivering results quickly, listening to feedback from teammates and customers, and learning from mistakes. You practice agility by working with others, trying out new ways to solve problems, and reflecting on what works and what doesn’t, both on your own and with your team. The main components of agility—adaptability, delivering working results, interactions with individuals, customer collaboration, and failing fast—are all about being flexible, responsive, and willing to learn and change as you go.

At different levels, practicing agility looks a bit different. At the first level, you’re just starting out: you know why agility matters, you’re willing to try it, and you’re beginning to practice with others. You’re learning the basics and reflecting on your experiences. At the second level, you’re making small changes to your habits, like being more open to feedback and adjusting your actions based on what you learn. You’re starting to see positive results and are involving others more in your decisions. By the third level, agility becomes a natural part of how you work—you adapt quickly, collaborate easily, deliver results often, and help others do the same. You set an example for your team, showing how to be flexible and learn from both successes and failures, and you help create a culture where everyone can keep getting better together.

column12: Adaptability is the ability to adjust your thoughts, actions, and plans when faced with unexpected changes or challenges. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like staying open-minded, being willing to try new approaches, learning from mistakes, and encouraging others to embrace change. Adaptability is a component of Agility, Decision-Making, and Continuous Growth practices, as it helps individuals respond quickly to new situations, make better choices in uncertain circumstances, and keep improving over time. It is assessed and practiced through questions like, "When events don't turn out how I expected, I am quick to adjust the path I am taking, and I encourage others to do so too," which measure how well someone can shift their approach and support others in doing the same., Delivering Working Results is a component of Agility practice that focuses on consistently providing usable outcomes to users and customers as early and often as possible. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like valuing quick feedback, prioritizing tasks that lead to tangible progress, and being open to adapting based on real-world input. People who succeed in Delivering Working Results are proactive in sharing their work, seek feedback early, and are comfortable making adjustments to improve outcomes. This component is closely tied to Agility because it emphasizes responsiveness, learning from experience, and continuous improvement. It is assessed and practiced through questions like "I prioritize getting the results into the hands of users and customers so that I can get feedback before I adjust," which encourages individuals to focus on delivering value quickly and iteratively, a core principle of agile methodologies., Interactions with Individuals is a component of Agility practice. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like valuing collaboration, seeking input from others, and adapting plans based on team feedback rather than relying solely on perfect, pre-made strategies. People who succeed in this component are open to others’ perspectives, communicate effectively, and are willing to adjust their actions in response to group needs. Interactions with Individuals supports Agility by encouraging flexible, team-based decision-making, which helps groups respond quickly to changing situations. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like “I prioritize figuring out decisions with my teammates instead of developing perfect plans before I act,” which measures a person’s willingness to collaborate and adapt in real time., Customer Collaboration is a component of Agility practice that focuses on actively engaging with customers throughout a project to ensure their needs and feedback shape the direction of the work. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like valuing ongoing communication, being open to change based on customer input, and prioritizing customer satisfaction over rigidly following a predetermined plan. Succeeding in Customer Collaboration requires strong listening skills, adaptability, and a willingness to build trust with customers by involving them in decision-making. It is closely related to Agility practices because it emphasizes flexibility, responsiveness, and continuous improvement based on real-world feedback. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like "I work with and talk to customers in order to determine the direction, rather than try to answer all questions up front," which evaluates how well individuals or teams involve customers in shaping outcomes rather than relying solely on initial assumptions or plans., Failing Fast is a component of Agility and Continuous Growth practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like viewing mistakes and setbacks as valuable learning opportunities, quickly identifying what doesn’t work, and adapting strategies based on feedback. People who succeed in Failing Fast are open to experimentation, resilient in the face of failure, and proactive in making changes to improve outcomes. Failing Fast is closely related to Agility, as it encourages rapid iteration and flexibility, and to Continuous Growth, as it fosters a mindset of ongoing learning and improvement. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like “I rely on failing as the opportunity to learn what I can do differently in the future,” which helps individuals reflect on how they use failure as a tool for personal and professional development.

column21: When you are at this practice level for Agility, it means that your agile habits—like adapting quickly, working well with others, delivering results often, collaborating with customers, and learning from mistakes—are becoming second nature when you work with others. You don’t have to think hard about being flexible or open to change; it just happens naturally. You’re also starting to set an example for your teammates, showing them what good agility looks like in real situations. You might even find yourself speaking up for agile ways of working, helping others see the value in being adaptable, working together, and learning from feedback. At this level, you’re not just practicing agility for yourself—you’re helping to build a team or group culture where everyone can respond quickly to change, learn from what doesn’t work, and keep improving together.

column3: Continuous Growth is a practice that means always trying to get better at what you do, not just by learning new facts or skills, but by working with others, reflecting on your actions, and making intentional choices to improve. Imagine it like tending a garden with friends: you never finish growing the plants, but you keep watering, pruning, and learning together, and over time, your habits become stronger and more natural. This practice is built on beliefs and behaviors like active listening, patience, adaptability, esteem, failing fast, self-awareness, feedback, reflection, and trust. These are like the tools and seeds you use in your garden—each one helps you and your team grow together. Unlike a skill, which is like learning to use a shovel (something you can master and use alone to make a specific thing), a practice is about how you show up with others and how you keep growing as a person and teammate. In tech jobs like project management, agile coaching, or UX, continuous growth is important because it helps teams adapt to change, learn from mistakes, and build trust, which leads to better results and happier teams. Both skills and practices need to be used regularly or they fade away, and both require you to want to get better. Success in any job comes from using your skills to get things done and practicing continuous growth to keep improving with your team.
column4: The practice of Continuous Growth is important to cross-functional teamwork in a lot of ways. In teams where people have different roles—like project managers, designers, researchers, writers, developers, and more—Continuous Growth means everyone is always learning, improving, and helping each other get better. This practice helps teams stay flexible and open to new ideas, which is key when working with people who have different skills and perspectives. For example, if a designer learns from a developer about technical limits, or a project manager learns from a researcher about user needs, the whole team gets stronger. Continuous Growth also builds trust, because team members see that everyone is willing to learn from mistakes, listen to feedback, and support each other’s progress. This makes it easier to solve problems together, adapt to changes, and create better results for everyone involved.
column5:
Continuous Growth is similar to other practices like Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Agility because it’s all about working with others, building trust, and always trying to get better at what you do. Like other practices, it’s not something you ever “finish”—you keep building it over time by reflecting on your actions, listening to feedback, and working with others to improve. It shares components like Active Listening, Patience, Adaptability, Esteem, Feedback, and Trust with other practices, which all help you connect with people, handle challenges, and support your team. But unlike skills, which are about what you can do or make (like managing a project or designing a user experience), practices are about your beliefs, behaviors, and how you show up with others—they’re measured by how you and others see your growth, not by a finished product.

What makes Continuous Growth unique is that it’s all about never stopping your personal development—always learning, reflecting, and improving, no matter how good you get. It includes special components like Failing Fast and Reflection, which encourage you to see mistakes as learning opportunities and to regularly look inward to understand yourself better. This practice is about being open to change, learning from every experience, and helping others grow too, making it a lifelong journey rather than a box you can check off.
column6:
This practice is carried out through a set of behaviors and beliefs that help you keep learning and improving, especially when working with others. Continuous Growth means you’re always trying to get better—not just at your job, but at how you listen, adapt, and support your team. You do this by practicing things like Active Listening (really paying attention to others), Patience (not rushing or getting frustrated), Adaptability (being open to change), Esteem (valuing yourself and others), Failing Fast (learning from mistakes), Self-Awareness (knowing your strengths and weaknesses), Feedback (asking for and using advice), Reflection (thinking about what you did and how you can improve), and Trust (believing in yourself and your team). These components work together to help you grow, not just by yourself, but with the people around you. You practice Continuous Growth by making a choice to try these behaviors, reflecting on how you’re doing, and being open to feedback from others.

At different levels, Continuous Growth looks a little different. At the first level, you might not know what makes up the practice or why it matters. At the next level, you start to understand the components, but you might not be willing to try them yet. As you move up, you begin to practice these habits, even if you stop sometimes or find it hard. When you reach higher levels, you’re not just trying these behaviors—you’re reflecting on your progress, making changes, and your positive habits start to feel natural. Eventually, Continuous Growth becomes part of who you are, and others notice how you help everyone improve together. It’s not about being perfect, but about always being willing to learn, adapt, and support your team’s growth.

column12: Active Listening is a component of Service Leadership, Decision-Making, Psychological Safety, Continuous Growth, and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like giving full attention to the speaker, withholding judgment, reflecting back what has been said, and acknowledging others’ perspectives before responding. Active Listening helps someone succeed by building trust, clarifying understanding, and encouraging open communication, which are essential for effective leadership, sound decision-making, creating safe environments, ongoing personal development, and empowering others. It is assessed and practiced through questions like “I repeat back what people have shared and acknowledge their views before responding,” which checks for the ability to listen attentively, confirm understanding, and show respect for others’ input., Patience is a component of Continuous Growth, Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like the ability to pause before reacting, wait for the right moment or for others to catch up, and exercise self-control even in challenging situations. Patience helps someone succeed by allowing them to thoughtfully consider their actions, support others’ development, and persist through difficulties without frustration. It is closely related to the associated practices because continuous growth requires time and perseverance, service leadership involves understanding and supporting others’ needs, empowerment means giving space for others to learn, and ownership requires managing one’s emotions and responses. Patience is assessed and practiced through questions like “I have the ability to pause, wait for alignment, exercise self-control,” which encourage reflection on how well someone manages their impulses and supports group progress., Adaptability is the ability to adjust your thoughts, actions, and plans when faced with unexpected changes or challenges. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like staying open-minded, being willing to try new approaches, learning from mistakes, and encouraging others to embrace change. Adaptability is a component of Agility, Decision-Making, and Continuous Growth practices, as it helps individuals respond quickly to new situations, make better choices in uncertain circumstances, and keep improving over time. It is assessed and practiced through questions like, "When events don't turn out how I expected, I am quick to adjust the path I am taking, and I encourage others to do so too," which measure how well someone can shift their approach and support others in doing the same., Esteem is a component of Ownership, Empowerment, and Continuous Growth practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like recognizing and valuing your own abilities, as well as appreciating the contributions of others, regardless of their skill level. People who succeed in Esteem are confident in their strengths, encourage others, and foster a positive team environment where everyone feels valued. Esteem is closely related to the associated practices because it helps individuals take responsibility for their growth (Ownership), feel empowered to contribute (Empowerment), and support ongoing improvement for themselves and others (Continuous Growth). Esteem is assessed and practiced through questions that ask individuals to reflect on how they view their own and others’ abilities as meaningful contributions to the team, and whether they believe everyone brings value, no matter their skill level., Failing Fast is a component of Agility and Continuous Growth practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like viewing mistakes and setbacks as valuable learning opportunities, quickly identifying what doesn’t work, and adapting strategies based on feedback. People who succeed in Failing Fast are open to experimentation, resilient in the face of failure, and proactive in making changes to improve outcomes. Failing Fast is closely related to Agility, as it encourages rapid iteration and flexibility, and to Continuous Growth, as it fosters a mindset of ongoing learning and improvement. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like “I rely on failing as the opportunity to learn what I can do differently in the future,” which helps individuals reflect on how they use failure as a tool for personal and professional development., Self-Awareness is a component of Continuous Growth and Service Leadership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like recognizing your own emotions, understanding your strengths and weaknesses, and being mindful of how your actions impact others. People who succeed in Self-Awareness are open to feedback, reflect on their experiences, and are willing to adjust their behavior to improve themselves and their relationships. Self-Awareness is closely connected to Continuous Growth because it helps individuals identify areas for personal development, and it supports Service Leadership by fostering empathy and responsible decision-making. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like "I actively pay attention to how my behaviors affect others so that I can reflect on how to work on changing my own behaviors," which encourages ongoing reflection and intentional self-improvement., Feedback is a component of Service Leadership, Decision-Making, and Continuous Growth practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like actively seeking, receiving, and using input from others to improve performance, make better decisions, and foster personal and team development. People who succeed in Feedback are open-minded, willing to listen, and able to reflect on both positive and constructive criticism to adapt and grow. Feedback is closely related to Service Leadership because it helps leaders understand the needs of those they serve, to Decision-Making because it provides valuable information for making informed choices, and to Continuous Growth because it drives ongoing learning and improvement. Feedback is assessed and practiced through questions like "I rely on user-centered and team-centered feedback on a continuous basis to learn how I need to improve," which encourages individuals to reflect on how consistently they use feedback to guide their actions and development., Reflection is a component of Continuous Growth practice that involves regularly examining your own thoughts, emotions, and actions to understand how you respond to situations and where you can improve. This component includes beliefs and behaviors like being honest with yourself, recognizing your strengths and weaknesses, and being open to learning from your experiences. Reflection helps you become more self-aware and intentional about your personal development, which is essential for continuous improvement. It is closely tied to Continuous Growth because looking inward and evaluating your behavior allows you to set goals and make changes that support ongoing learning and progress. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like "I constantly look inwards and evaluate my emotions and how I showed up, making a note about where / how I could improve," which encourages you to actively engage in self-evaluation and identify areas for growth., Trust is a component of Psychological Safety, Service Leadership, Empowerment, Continuous Growth, and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like relying on the integrity and abilities of oneself and others, being honest, keeping commitments, and showing vulnerability by admitting mistakes or asking for help. Trust helps people feel safe to share ideas, take risks, and collaborate, which strengthens team relationships and supports growth and accountability. It is closely linked to psychological safety, as trust allows people to express themselves without fear, and it is essential for leaders who serve and empower others, as well as for individuals who take ownership and strive for continuous improvement. Trust is assessed and practiced through questions like "I value trust in myself and trust in others to carry out work," which encourages reflection on personal and team trustworthiness and the willingness to depend on others.

column15: When you are at this practice level for Continuous Growth, it means you are just starting out and may not even know what Continuous Growth really is or what it looks like in action. You might not be aware of the beliefs and behaviors that make up this practice, like being open to feedback, reflecting on your actions, or learning from mistakes. At this stage, you probably haven’t tried to practice Continuous Growth, or if you did, you stopped quickly. You might not feel ready or willing to work on things like active listening, patience, adaptability, or self-awareness. In simple terms, you’re at the very beginning—like standing at the starting line of a race, not sure what the course looks like or how to run it yet.
column16:
1. You believe it's valuable to practice the practice
2. You are willing to practice it
3. You have knowledge about the components of the practice
4. You've started practicing with others and reflecting
column17: When you are at this practice level for Continuous Growth, it means you not only see the value in always learning and improving, but you’re also willing to put in the effort to do it. You understand what makes up Continuous Growth—like listening carefully to others (Active Listening), being patient, adapting to change, valuing yourself and others (Esteem), learning from mistakes (Failing Fast), knowing yourself (Self-Awareness), giving and receiving feedback, reflecting on your actions, and building trust. You’ve started practicing these things with other people, not just by yourself, and you take time to think about how it’s going. This level is about more than just knowing what Continuous Growth is; it’s about actually trying it out with others, noticing what works, and being open to learning from your experiences and the feedback you get.
column18:
1. You've seen growth in your own practice through reflection
2. You are starting to work on changing habits
column19: When you are at this practice level for Continuous Growth, it means you’ve started to notice how you’re improving by looking back and thinking about your actions and choices. You’re not just doing things the same way every time—you’re beginning to reflect on what’s working and what isn’t, and you’re open to making changes. For example, if you realize you get stressed when managing a project, you might pause to think about why, and then try a new way to organize your tasks. You’re also starting to work on changing your habits, like being more patient with teammates, listening more carefully, or being open to feedback. This level is about being honest with yourself, learning from your experiences, and taking small steps to grow, even if it feels uncomfortable at first. It’s not about being perfect, but about being willing to learn, adapt, and keep moving forward.
column20:
1. Your habits are starting to become automatic in your practice with others
2. You’re so practiced in it, that you are a role model to others in the practice
3. You can and do advocate for the practice when working with others
column21: When you are at this practice level for Continuous Growth, your positive habits—like listening carefully, being patient, adapting to change, valuing yourself and others, learning from mistakes, being self-aware, seeking feedback, reflecting on your actions, and building trust—are starting to feel natural when you work with others. You don’t have to think about them all the time; they’re becoming part of how you act every day. For example, just like brushing your teeth becomes automatic after doing it for years, these behaviors start to happen without much effort. You’re not just learning these habits—you’re living them, and people around you can see the difference. This means you’re on your way to becoming someone others look up to for how you keep growing and helping everyone improve together.

column3: Decision-Making as a practice means working with others to make choices, and it’s something you keep getting better at over time, but never fully master. It’s built on beliefs and behaviors like listening, being open to change, making fair judgments, keeping promises, building agreement, getting everyone’s support, setting clear expectations, giving and receiving feedback, and knowing when to pause and reflect. Imagine a team steering a ship together—everyone’s input helps decide which way to go, and you need to keep talking, listening, and adjusting as the weather changes. You can’t measure Decision-Making by a finished product, but by how people feel about your choices and how you feel about your own process. It’s a choice you make every day, and you get better by thinking about your actions and talking with others. In tech jobs, this practice is key because it helps teams solve problems, adapt to new challenges, and build trust, which leads to better results and happier teams. If you stop practicing, you can lose your touch, just like with any skill, and you need to want to do it for it to work.
column4:
The practice of Decision-Making is important to cross-functional teamwork in a lot of ways. In teams where people have different roles—like project managers, designers, developers, researchers, and more—everyone brings their own ideas and expertise. Decision-Making helps the team choose the best path forward by making sure everyone’s voice is heard, weighing different options, and agreeing on what to do next. This is especially important because, in cross-functional teams, no one person has all the answers. Good Decision-Making means the team can move forward together, avoid confusion, and solve problems faster.

For example, a project manager needs Decision-Making to set priorities and keep the project on track. A designer uses it to pick the best design based on feedback from users and teammates. A developer relies on it to choose the right technical solution. Researchers use Decision-Making to decide which questions to ask or which findings to focus on. Even roles like QA analysts, product owners, and account managers need to make decisions that affect the whole team. When everyone practices Decision-Making well, the team can handle changes, learn from mistakes, and make sure everyone is committed to the plan. This leads to better products, happier customers, and a stronger team overall.
column5:
Decision-Making is similar to other practices because it’s all about working with people, building habits over time, and being intentional about how you act and think—just like practices such as Service Leadership or Ownership. Unlike skills, which are about what you can do on your own and can be measured by how many times you’ve done something (like making a project plan or writing code), practices like Decision-Making are measured by how you and others see your actions and beliefs. Decision-Making shares components with other practices, such as Active Listening, Adaptability, Commitment, Feedback, and Pause, which are also found in Service Leadership, Continuous Growth, and Empowerment. For example, both Decision-Making and Service Leadership require you to listen carefully and give feedback, but Decision-Making is especially focused on how you make choices with others, get buy-in, and set clear expectations so everyone is on the same page.

What makes Decision-Making unique is that it centers on how you form judgments, build consensus, and make choices that affect a group, not just yourself. It’s not just about picking the best option—it’s about making sure everyone’s voice is heard, checking your own assumptions, and making decisions that people can support and commit to. The components like Judgment Formation, Consensus, and Buy-In are especially important in Decision-Making, because they help you make flexible, fair, and well-supported choices, even when things change or get tough. This focus on group input and shared responsibility is what sets Decision-Making apart from other practices and from skills, which are more about what you can do on your own.
column6:
This practice is carried out through a set of beliefs and behaviors that help people make good choices together. Decision-Making involves listening carefully to others (active listening), being willing to change your mind when new information comes up (adaptability), thinking through your options (judgment formation), keeping your promises (commitment), working to get everyone’s agreement (consensus), making sure everyone feels included and heard (buy-in), clearly explaining what’s expected (expectation setting), asking for and using feedback, and taking time to pause and reflect before making a choice. These components work together to create an environment where decisions are made thoughtfully, with input from everyone involved, and where people feel respected and valued.

At different levels, the practice looks a bit different. At Level 1, you’re just starting out: you know why decision-making is important, you’re open to trying it, and you’re learning the basics like listening and including others. At Level 2, you’re becoming more aware of how your choices affect others, you’re reflecting on your decisions, and you’re starting to change your habits—like pausing to consider different viewpoints and making sure you follow through on what you say. At Level 3, these habits become natural; you include others and listen without thinking about it, people trust your decisions, and you help others get better at decision-making too. Each level builds on the last, helping you grow from a beginner to someone who leads and supports good decision-making in any group.
column7:
Practices:
1. A practice requires working with other people
2. A practice never stops being built; you never become an expert in a practice, but the behaviors of a practice become more permanent over time
3. A practice is rooted in beliefs and behaviors
4. A practice can be measured through what other people believe about you
5. A practice cannot be concretely measured through deliverables
6. A practice is an intentional choice someone makes to do
7. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting to yourself
8. A practice can be measured through what you believe about yourself
9. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting with others
Skills:
1. A skill enables you to make a deliverable
2. A skill is measured through how many times you have applied it
3. A skill is rooted in  knowledge and theory
4. A skill applies to a specific activity
5. A skill is expected to be performed in specific job functions
6. A skill is something someone can become an expert in it with a lot of experience
7. A skill you can perform yourself
8. A skill can be practiced by applying it to work you deliver or duties you perform
9. A skill is required in specific jobs, people may not have a choice to perform the skill if the job requires them
10. A skill gets practiced by doing work and performing duties

What they both have in common: 
1. A practice and a skill can be lost if  not continued to be practiced
2. A practice and a skill require willingness to perform

column12: Active Listening is a component of Service Leadership, Decision-Making, Psychological Safety, Continuous Growth, and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like giving full attention to the speaker, withholding judgment, reflecting back what has been said, and acknowledging others’ perspectives before responding. Active Listening helps someone succeed by building trust, clarifying understanding, and encouraging open communication, which are essential for effective leadership, sound decision-making, creating safe environments, ongoing personal development, and empowering others. It is assessed and practiced through questions like “I repeat back what people have shared and acknowledge their views before responding,” which checks for the ability to listen attentively, confirm understanding, and show respect for others’ input., Adaptability is the ability to adjust your thoughts, actions, and plans when faced with unexpected changes or challenges. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like staying open-minded, being willing to try new approaches, learning from mistakes, and encouraging others to embrace change. Adaptability is a component of Agility, Decision-Making, and Continuous Growth practices, as it helps individuals respond quickly to new situations, make better choices in uncertain circumstances, and keep improving over time. It is assessed and practiced through questions like, "When events don't turn out how I expected, I am quick to adjust the path I am taking, and I encourage others to do so too," which measure how well someone can shift their approach and support others in doing the same., Judgment Formation is a component of Decision-Making practice that involves the beliefs and behaviors necessary to make sound, flexible decisions in changing situations. This component requires individuals to assess information, weigh alternatives, and adapt their choices as new circumstances arise, demonstrating open-mindedness and the ability to reconsider previous decisions. Successful Judgment Formation depends on critical thinking, self-awareness, and the willingness to learn from experience, which helps people avoid rigid thinking and make better choices. It is closely tied to Decision-Making because effective decisions rely on the ability to form judgments that are responsive to context and evidence. This component is assessed and practiced through questions that ask individuals to reflect on their ability to change decisions when situations change and to operate with flexibility, such as, "I am able to change my decisions in the context of situations that change, and operate with flexibility in the decisions I make based on judgment calls.", Commitment is a component of Ownership, Service Leadership, and Decision-Making practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like keeping promises, following through on responsibilities, and being honest about what you can and cannot do. People who succeed in Commitment are reliable, communicate clearly if they need to change an agreement, and only take on tasks they are sure they can complete. Commitment supports Ownership by encouraging accountability, strengthens Service Leadership by building trust with others, and improves Decision-Making by ensuring choices are made with integrity and follow-through. Commitment is assessed and practiced through questions that ask if you do what you said you would do by the agreed time, if you make new agreements when needed, and if you only agree to things you can truly commit to., Consensus is a component of Decision-Making practice that involves the belief that group decisions are stronger when everyone’s voice is heard and valued. This component is about behaviors like actively listening to team members, encouraging open discussion, and working together to find solutions that everyone can support, even if it’s not their first choice. People who succeed in consensus-building are patient, respectful, and willing to compromise, understanding that the best decisions often come from collective knowledge and shared agreement. Consensus relates to Decision-Making because it ensures that choices are made with input from all members, leading to more commitment and better outcomes. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I rely on the voice of the team to build a consensus based on knowledge they have at the moment," which checks if someone values and uses group input when making decisions., Buy-In is a component of Decision-Making practice that involves beliefs and behaviors like actively seeking input from others, being open to feedback, and ensuring that everyone affected by a decision feels heard and valued. People who succeed in Buy-In demonstrate empathy, patience, and a willingness to check their own assumptions, which helps create an environment where others are more likely to support and engage with decisions. This component is closely related to Decision-Making because effective decisions often require the commitment and agreement of those involved, making it essential to foster trust and collaboration. Buy-In is assessed and practiced through questions like "I check my assumptions when in the process of making decisions to give people time to reflect and respond whenever possible," which encourages individuals to pause, reflect, and invite others into the decision-making process, ultimately leading to better outcomes and stronger group cohesion., Expectation Setting is a component of Ownership and Decision-Making practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like clearly communicating goals, roles, and responsibilities so that everyone understands what is expected of them and can take responsibility for their part of the work. People who succeed in Expectation Setting are good at making sure everyone is on the same page, asking questions to clarify any confusion, and making sure that expectations are realistic and agreed upon by all involved. Expectation Setting is closely related to Ownership because when expectations are clear, people are more likely to take responsibility for their work, and it supports Decision-Making by providing a shared understanding that guides choices and actions. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like “I work to build a shared understanding of outcomes so that people can own work,” which checks whether someone is actively making expectations clear and encouraging others to take ownership. Reputable sources highlight that effective expectation setting leads to better teamwork, accountability, and project success., Feedback is a component of Service Leadership, Decision-Making, and Continuous Growth practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like actively seeking, receiving, and using input from others to improve performance, make better decisions, and foster personal and team development. People who succeed in Feedback are open-minded, willing to listen, and able to reflect on both positive and constructive criticism to adapt and grow. Feedback is closely related to Service Leadership because it helps leaders understand the needs of those they serve, to Decision-Making because it provides valuable information for making informed choices, and to Continuous Growth because it drives ongoing learning and improvement. Feedback is assessed and practiced through questions like "I rely on user-centered and team-centered feedback on a continuous basis to learn how I need to improve," which encourages individuals to reflect on how consistently they use feedback to guide their actions and development., Pause is a component of Empowerment, Psychological Safety, Decision-Making, and Service Leadership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like intentionally creating space in conversations for reflection, allowing others to process information, and encouraging everyone to contribute their thoughts without feeling rushed. Pausing helps people feel valued and heard, which builds trust and confidence in group settings. It is closely related to the associated practices because it empowers individuals to speak up, fosters a safe environment for sharing ideas, supports thoughtful decision-making, and demonstrates a service-oriented approach to leadership. The component is assessed and practiced through questions like "I actively prioritize pausing in conversations to ensure that people in the room are able to provide input and voice," which measures how well someone uses pauses to include others and promote open dialogue.

column15: When you are at this practice level for Decision-Making, it means you are not yet aware of what makes up the practice. In simple terms, you might not know what good decision-making looks like, what beliefs and behaviors are important, or how it connects to working with others. You haven’t started thinking about how your choices affect your team, and you might not realize that decision-making is more than just picking an option—it’s about listening, being flexible, building agreement, and making sure everyone understands what’s expected. At this stage, you haven’t begun to reflect on your own decision-making or ask others for feedback, so you’re not yet building the habits that help you grow in this area.

column17: When you are at this practice level for Decision-Making, it means you’re just starting to build the habits and beliefs that help you make good choices with others. You understand why it’s important to practice decision-making, you’re open to trying it, and you know the basic parts that make up good decision-making—like listening to others, being flexible, and making sure everyone’s voice is heard. You’ve begun to practice these behaviors with your team or group, and you take time to think about how it went, either by yourself or with others. At this stage, you’re not expected to be perfect or always get it right, but you’re showing that you care about making decisions in a thoughtful, inclusive way and are willing to learn and grow from each experience.
column18:

column19: When you are at this practice level for Decision-Making, it means you’re starting to notice how your choices and actions affect yourself and others, and you’re reflecting on your decisions to learn and grow. You’re beginning to change old habits and try new ways of making decisions, like listening more carefully to others, being open to feedback, and thinking about how your choices impact your team. At this stage, you’re not just acting on instinct—you’re pausing to consider different perspectives, checking your assumptions, and working to build agreement with others. You’re also learning to adjust your decisions when things change, and you’re making an effort to clearly communicate expectations and follow through on your commitments. This level is all about being aware, open to growth, and intentionally practicing better decision-making with the people around you.
column20:
1. Your habits are starting to become automatic in your practice with others
2. You’re so practiced in it, that you are a role model to others in the practice
3. You can and do advocate for the practice when working with others
column21: When you are at this practice level for Decision-Making, your habits around making decisions with others are becoming second nature. You don’t have to think hard about including people, listening to their ideas, or making sure everyone understands what’s happening—these things just happen naturally when you work with others. People start to notice that you’re good at making fair and thoughtful decisions, and you become someone others look up to for how you handle choices in a group. You’re also comfortable speaking up for the importance of good decision-making, encouraging others to use these habits, and making sure everyone’s voice is heard. This means you’re not just practicing decision-making for yourself, but you’re also helping others get better at it too.

column3: Empowerment is a practice that means helping yourself and others feel confident and able to make choices, take action, and contribute to a team. Imagine a sports team where everyone cheers each other on, trusts each other to make plays, and celebrates even small wins together—empowerment is like being on that team, where everyone’s voice matters and everyone helps each other grow. This practice is built on beliefs and behaviors like respect, encouragement, trust, and letting go of control, and it never stops growing because it depends on how you and others feel and act together. You can’t measure empowerment by counting finished projects, but you can see it in how people feel about working with you and how you feel about yourself. Skills like Project Management, Agile Coaching, or Data Analysis are different—they’re like tools you use to build something specific, and you can become an expert by practicing them over and over, often by yourself. Both practices and skills need you to keep using them, or you’ll lose them, and both require you to want to get better. In tech jobs, empowerment helps teams work better together, solve problems creatively, and support each other, while skills help you get the actual work done. Success in any job comes from using both: the practice of empowerment makes the team strong and positive, and the skills make sure the work gets done well.
column4: The practice of Empowerment is important to cross-functional teamwork in a lot of ways. Empowerment means giving people the confidence, trust, and freedom to make decisions and take action in their roles. In cross-functional teams—where people from different backgrounds like project management, product ownership, UX design, development, and more work together—empowerment helps everyone feel valued and motivated to contribute their best ideas. When team members are empowered, they’re more likely to speak up, share creative solutions, and take responsibility for their work. This leads to better problem-solving, faster progress, and a stronger sense of ownership across the team. Empowerment also builds trust, because people know their opinions matter and that they can rely on each other. For example, a developer who feels empowered might suggest a new way to solve a technical problem, while a UX designer who feels trusted might try a bold new design. In the end, empowerment helps everyone grow, learn from mistakes, and celebrate successes together, making the team more effective and united.
column5:
Empowerment is similar and different to other practices in many ways. Like other practices, Empowerment is about how you work with people, not just what you do by yourself. It’s built on beliefs and behaviors, such as trust, respect, and encouragement, and you never really “finish” getting better at it—you keep growing by reflecting on yourself and with others. Empowerment shares components like Esteem, Active Listening, Removing Ego, and Patience with practices such as Ownership, Service Leadership, and Continuous Growth, which all focus on building positive team environments and helping everyone feel valued. However, unlike skills—which are about what you can do, like making a project plan or coding, and can be measured by what you deliver—practices like Empowerment are measured by how others feel about working with you and how you make them feel about themselves.

What makes Empowerment unique is that it’s all about giving people the confidence and freedom to make their own choices, try new things, and learn from mistakes without fear. Its special components, like Celebration, Autonomy, Letting Go of Control, and Approach, focus on helping others feel trusted and supported to take action on their own. Empowerment stands out because it’s not just about being a good teammate or leader—it’s about making sure everyone has a voice, feels capable, and is encouraged to grow, even if things don’t go perfectly. This creates a team where people are excited to contribute, take risks, and support each other’s success.
column6:
This practice is carried out through a set of beliefs and behaviors that help people feel confident, valued, and able to make their own choices. Empowerment is not just about giving people tasks—it’s about building a team where everyone feels respected, listened to, and trusted. You do this by showing esteem (valuing yourself and others), practicing active listening (really paying attention to what people say), removing ego (knowing you don’t have all the answers), offering encouragement, being patient, showing respect, celebrating efforts, giving autonomy (letting people make decisions), letting go of control, acknowledging others’ perspectives, pausing to let everyone share, adapting your approach to support others, and building trust. These components work together to create a positive environment where people want to contribute and grow.

At different levels, empowerment looks a little different. At the first level, you’re just starting out: you know what empowerment is, you’re willing to try it, and you begin practicing these behaviors with others, reflecting on how it goes. At the second level, you notice that your actions are making a difference—you’re breaking old habits, supporting your teammates more, and helping others feel confident and capable. At the third level, empowerment becomes second nature. You naturally encourage and trust others, celebrate their efforts, and help create a team where everyone feels they belong and can succeed. At this stage, you set an example for others and speak up for the importance of empowerment in your team.
column7:
Practices:
1. A practice requires working with other people
2. A practice never stops being built; you never become an expert in a practice, but the behaviors of a practice become more permanent over time
3. A practice is rooted in beliefs and behaviors
4. A practice can be measured through what other people believe about you
5. A practice cannot be concretely measured through deliverables
6. A practice is an intentional choice someone makes to do
7. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting to yourself
8. A practice can be measured through what you believe about yourself
9. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting with others
Skills:
1. A skill enables you to make a deliverable
2. A skill is measured through how many times you have applied it
3. A skill is rooted in  knowledge and theory
4. A skill applies to a specific activity
5. A skill is expected to be performed in specific job functions
6. A skill is something someone can become an expert in it with a lot of experience
7. A skill you can perform yourself
8. A skill can be practiced by applying it to work you deliver or duties you perform
9. A skill is required in specific jobs, people may not have a choice to perform the skill if the job requires them
10. A skill gets practiced by doing work and performing duties

What they both have in common: 
1. A practice and a skill can be lost if  not continued to be practiced
2. A practice and a skill require willingness to perform
column8: Activities that involve interacting with other people that you always work on.
column9: Empowerment Self Assessment,Team Agile Maturity Assessment
column12: Esteem is a component of Ownership, Empowerment, and Continuous Growth practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like recognizing and valuing your own abilities, as well as appreciating the contributions of others, regardless of their skill level. People who succeed in Esteem are confident in their strengths, encourage others, and foster a positive team environment where everyone feels valued. Esteem is closely related to the associated practices because it helps individuals take responsibility for their growth (Ownership), feel empowered to contribute (Empowerment), and support ongoing improvement for themselves and others (Continuous Growth). Esteem is assessed and practiced through questions that ask individuals to reflect on how they view their own and others’ abilities as meaningful contributions to the team, and whether they believe everyone brings value, no matter their skill level., Active Listening is a component of Service Leadership, Decision-Making, Psychological Safety, Continuous Growth, and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like giving full attention to the speaker, withholding judgment, reflecting back what has been said, and acknowledging others’ perspectives before responding. Active Listening helps someone succeed by building trust, clarifying understanding, and encouraging open communication, which are essential for effective leadership, sound decision-making, creating safe environments, ongoing personal development, and empowering others. It is assessed and practiced through questions like “I repeat back what people have shared and acknowledge their views before responding,” which checks for the ability to listen attentively, confirm understanding, and show respect for others’ input., Removing Ego is a component of Ownership, Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Psychological Safety practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like recognizing that you do not have all the answers, valuing the contributions of others regardless of their experience, and being open to learning from teammates. Removing Ego helps someone succeed by fostering humility, encouraging collaboration, and creating an environment where everyone feels comfortable sharing ideas. It is closely related to the associated practices because it supports taking responsibility (Ownership), serving and uplifting others (Service Leadership), enabling others to contribute (Empowerment), and making it safe for everyone to speak up (Psychological Safety). This component is assessed and practiced through questions that ask whether you prioritize the mindset that you do not have all the right answers in teamwork and remind yourself that others can produce great things, even when they don’t have experience., Encouragement is a component of Ownership and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like offering positive reinforcement, expressing support, and motivating others, especially when they face setbacks or failures. Encouragement helps people feel valued and confident, which increases their willingness to take initiative and responsibility for their actions. It is closely related to Ownership, as it fosters a sense of personal investment, and to Empowerment, as it builds the confidence needed to try new things and persist through challenges. Encouragement is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I provide positive reinforcement to others, even when they have tried and failed," which measures how often someone supports and uplifts others in difficult moments., Patience is a component of Continuous Growth, Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like the ability to pause before reacting, wait for the right moment or for others to catch up, and exercise self-control even in challenging situations. Patience helps someone succeed by allowing them to thoughtfully consider their actions, support others’ development, and persist through difficulties without frustration. It is closely related to the associated practices because continuous growth requires time and perseverance, service leadership involves understanding and supporting others’ needs, empowerment means giving space for others to learn, and ownership requires managing one’s emotions and responses. Patience is assessed and practiced through questions like “I have the ability to pause, wait for alignment, exercise self-control,” which encourage reflection on how well someone manages their impulses and supports group progress., Respect is a component of Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like valuing the perspectives and experiences of others, listening actively, and acknowledging the strengths and wisdom that others bring, even when there are disagreements. Respect helps someone succeed by fostering trust, collaboration, and open-mindedness, which are essential for effective leadership, empowering others, and taking responsibility for shared goals. It is closely related to these practices because service leaders must honor those they serve, empowerment requires recognizing others’ contributions, and ownership involves appreciating diverse viewpoints. Respect is assessed and practiced through questions like “I recognize the wisdom of others. I acknowledge the ways other people are right even when there's disagreement,” which encourage self-reflection on how well one values and learns from others’ insights., Celebration is a component of Empowerment practice that involves recognizing and appreciating the efforts, achievements, and progress of individuals or teams, regardless of whether the outcome was perfect or even successful. This component includes beliefs and behaviors like expressing gratitude, acknowledging hard work, and publicly highlighting contributions, which help build confidence, motivation, and a sense of belonging within a group. Celebration supports empowerment by making people feel valued and encouraging them to take risks and learn from mistakes. It is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I recognize efforts in others on the team out loud and in public, even when they didn’t do something perfectly, or failed in something," which measures how openly and consistently individuals celebrate the efforts of others, reinforcing a positive and supportive team culture., Autonomy is a component of Empowerment practice that involves beliefs and behaviors like making independent choices, taking initiative, and trusting in one’s own ability to solve problems without relying on others for constant guidance. People who succeed in Autonomy are confident in their decision-making, willing to try new approaches, and comfortable learning from mistakes. This component is closely tied to Empowerment because when individuals are given the freedom to act on their own, they develop a stronger sense of ownership and motivation. Autonomy is assessed and practiced through questions that encourage individuals to figure things out independently and reinforce the belief that they are capable of doing so, such as telling them directly that you trust their abilities and expect them to solve problems on their own., Letting Go of Control is a component of Ownership and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like trusting others, accepting uncertainty, and allowing situations to unfold without always intervening or micromanaging. People who succeed in Letting Go of Control are able to recognize when their need to direct outcomes is unhelpful, and instead focus on influencing what they can while accepting what they cannot change. This approach helps build resilience, encourages collaboration, and fosters a sense of shared responsibility, which are key aspects of Ownership and Empowerment. Letting Go of Control is assessed and practiced through questions like "I prioritize letting events play out over trying to take control over outcomes," which encourages reflection on one’s ability to step back and allow others or circumstances to take their course., Acknowledgement is a component of Service Leadership, Ownership, and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like actively listening to others, recognizing the validity of their feelings or perspectives, and expressing understanding, even when you disagree or see things differently. Acknowledgement helps build trust, respect, and open communication, which are essential for effective teamwork and leadership. It is closely related to Service Leadership, where leaders serve others by valuing their input; Ownership, where individuals take responsibility for their interactions; and Empowerment, where people feel heard and respected, encouraging them to contribute more confidently. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I acknowledge the truth in others' words often, even when I think they could be wrong or have a different perspective," which measures how often someone recognizes and validates others' viewpoints, fostering a more inclusive and supportive environment., Pause is a component of Empowerment, Psychological Safety, Decision-Making, and Service Leadership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like intentionally creating space in conversations for reflection, allowing others to process information, and encouraging everyone to contribute their thoughts without feeling rushed. Pausing helps people feel valued and heard, which builds trust and confidence in group settings. It is closely related to the associated practices because it empowers individuals to speak up, fosters a safe environment for sharing ideas, supports thoughtful decision-making, and demonstrates a service-oriented approach to leadership. The component is assessed and practiced through questions like "I actively prioritize pausing in conversations to ensure that people in the room are able to provide input and voice," which measures how well someone uses pauses to include others and promote open dialogue., Approach is a component of Psychological Safety, Service Leadership, and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like actively seeking to understand others’ preferences and needs, rather than assuming your own way of support is best. People who succeed in Approach listen carefully, ask open-ended questions, and adapt their actions based on feedback, which helps build trust and respect within a group. Approach is closely related to Psychological Safety because it encourages open communication and reduces fear of judgment, to Service Leadership because it centers on serving others’ needs, and to Empowerment because it gives people a voice in how they are supported. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, “I ask others how they want to be served and supported instead of supporting them in the way I want to be supported,” which measures whether someone is prioritizing others’ perspectives and adapting their approach accordingly., Trust is a component of Psychological Safety, Service Leadership, Empowerment, Continuous Growth, and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like relying on the integrity and abilities of oneself and others, being honest, keeping commitments, and showing vulnerability by admitting mistakes or asking for help. Trust helps people feel safe to share ideas, take risks, and collaborate, which strengthens team relationships and supports growth and accountability. It is closely linked to psychological safety, as trust allows people to express themselves without fear, and it is essential for leaders who serve and empower others, as well as for individuals who take ownership and strive for continuous improvement. Trust is assessed and practiced through questions like "I value trust in myself and trust in others to carry out work," which encourages reflection on personal and team trustworthiness and the willingness to depend on others.
column13: I acknowledge the truth in others' words often, even when I think they could be wrong or have a different perspective, I repeat back what people have shared and acknowledge their views before responding, I ask others how they want to be served and supported instead of supporting them in the way I want to be supported., I want people to figure things out on their own, and believe in their capabilities to do so without my intervention, and tell them so, I recognize efforts in others on the team out loud and in public, even when they didn’t do something perfectly, or failed in something, I provide positive reinforcement to others, even when they have tried and failed, I encourage myself and others to see my abilities as a meaningful contribution to the team; I believe people bring value no matter what their skill level is, including myself, I prioritize letting events play out over trying to take control over outcomes, I have the ability to pause, wait for alignment, exercise self-control, I actively prioritize pausing in conversations to ensure that people in the room are able to provide input and voice, I prioritize the mindset that I do not have all of the right answers in teamwork, and tell myself that others can produce great things, even when they don’t have experience, I recognize the wisdom of others. I acknowledge the ways other people are right even when there's disagreement., I value trust in myself and trust in others to carry out work.
column14:
1. You are not aware of what makes up the practice
2. You are not willing to practice it
3. You tried to practice it, but stopped
column15: When you are at this practice level for Empowerment, it means you are just starting out and may not even know what Empowerment really is or what it looks like in action. You might not be aware of the beliefs and behaviors that make up Empowerment, like trusting others, encouraging your teammates, or letting people make their own choices. At this stage, you haven’t tried to practice Empowerment, or maybe you tried once but stopped. You might not feel ready or willing to give others more freedom or to celebrate their efforts, and you may not see how important it is to help others feel confident and valued in a group. This is a normal starting point—everyone begins here before they learn what Empowerment means and how it can make a team stronger and more positive.
column16:
1. You believe it's valuable to practice the practice
2. You are willing to practice it
3. You have knowledge about the components of the practice
4. You've started practicing with others and reflecting
column17: When you are at this practice level for Empowerment, it means you understand why Empowerment is important, you’re open to practicing it, and you know what makes up Empowerment—like Esteem, Active Listening, Removing Ego, Encouragement, Patience, Respect, Celebration, Autonomy, Letting Go of Control, Acknowledgement, Pause, Approach, and Trust. You’ve started to actually try these behaviors with others, not just by yourself, and you take time to think about how it went. For example, you might encourage a teammate who’s struggling, listen carefully to someone’s ideas without interrupting, or let someone else take the lead on a project. You also reflect on these moments, maybe by talking with others or thinking about what you did well and what you could do better. At this level, you’re not just learning about Empowerment—you’re putting it into action with real people and learning from your experiences.
column18:
1. You've seen growth in your own practice through reflection
2. You are starting to work on changing habits
column19: When you are at this practice level for Empowerment, it means you’re starting to notice positive changes in how you work with others because you’ve taken time to reflect on your actions and attitudes. You’re beginning to break old habits and try new ways of supporting and uplifting your teammates. At this stage, you’re not just thinking about your own growth—you’re also starting to help others feel confident, valued, and capable of making decisions. You might find yourself encouraging others, listening more carefully, and trusting people to take on responsibilities, even if things don’t go perfectly. This level is about building a team environment where everyone feels they can contribute, learn, and grow together.
column20:
1. Your habits are starting to become automatic in your practice with others
2. You’re so practiced in it, that you are a role model to others in the practice
3. You can and do advocate for the practice when working with others
column21: When you are at this practice level for Empowerment, it means that empowering others has become a natural part of how you work with people. You don’t have to think hard about it—it’s just what you do. Others notice this about you, and you set a positive example for your team. You’re not only good at empowering others, but you also speak up for the importance of empowerment when working with others. You encourage people to take initiative, trust their abilities, and make their own decisions. You celebrate their efforts, give them space to grow, and show respect for their ideas, even if they’re different from your own. At this level, you help create an environment where everyone feels valued, confident, and motivated to contribute.
