column3: Ownership as a practice means taking responsibility for your work and your role in a team, like being a gardener who cares for the whole garden, not just one plant. It’s about working with others, making intentional choices, and building trust, respect, and encouragement over time. You never fully “master” ownership; instead, you keep growing by reflecting on your actions, listening to feedback, and helping others take charge too. Components like Delegation, Growth Mindset, and Willingness are like the tools and seeds you use to help the garden thrive—trusting others, learning from mistakes, and letting go of control so everyone can grow. Ownership is measured by how you and others feel about your contributions, not by a checklist of tasks. In tech jobs, skills like Project Management or API design are like the gardening techniques you use to build things—these are specific, can be learned and measured, and you can become an expert with practice. Both ownership and skills need regular practice and willingness, or they fade away, just like a garden needs constant care. Success in any tech role comes from combining strong skills (what you do) with the ongoing practice of ownership (how you work with others and help the whole team succeed).
column4: The practice of Ownership is important to cross-functional teamwork in a lot of ways. When people in different roles—like project managers, designers, developers, analysts, and coaches—take ownership, it means they don’t just do their own tasks and walk away. Instead, they care about the whole team’s success and feel responsible for the outcome, not just their part. This helps everyone stay motivated, solve problems together, and support each other when things get tough. For example, if a developer notices a problem in the design, they speak up and help fix it, even if it’s not “their job.” Or if a project manager sees that someone is struggling, they step in to help or ask others to help, instead of ignoring it. Ownership also means trusting teammates, letting others lead sometimes, and being honest about mistakes so the team can learn and improve. When everyone practices ownership, the team works better together, gets more done, and creates better results for everyone.
column5:
Ownership is similar to other practices because it’s all about how you work with others, build trust, and keep growing your beliefs and behaviors over time. Like other practices, Ownership isn’t something you can just check off a list or measure by what you make; instead, it’s about how you show up, how others see you, and how you reflect on your actions with yourself and your team. For example, components like Trust, Respect, and Commitment are found in other practices too, such as Service Leadership and Empowerment, because they all focus on building strong relationships and helping everyone succeed together. But Ownership is different from skills like Project Management or Coding, which are about what you can do or make on your own and can be measured by how many times you’ve done them or what you’ve produced. Practices are about your mindset and how you interact with people, while skills are about what you can do by yourself.

What makes Ownership unique is that it’s all about taking responsibility for your actions and outcomes, and also making space for others to do the same. Its special components—like Delegation, Willingness, Letting Go of Control, and Transferring Power—are all about trusting others, sharing responsibility, and helping everyone feel empowered to lead and contribute. Unlike other practices that might focus more on serving others or making decisions, Ownership is about stepping up, being honest with yourself, and encouraging everyone to take part and grow together. It’s not just about doing your own part, but about making sure everyone has a chance to own their work and feel valued in the process.
column6:
This practice is carried out through a set of beliefs and behaviors that help people take responsibility and empower others in a group. Ownership is not just about doing your own work—it’s about sharing responsibility, trusting your teammates, and helping everyone feel like they can make a difference. The main components of Ownership include things like delegation (letting others take on tasks), having a growth mindset (believing you and others can improve), willingness (being open to others leading), letting go of control (not micromanaging), setting clear expectations, showing esteem and respect, practicing humility, acknowledging others, making commitments, removing ego, being honest with yourself, encouraging others, participating actively, being patient, transferring power, and building trust. Each of these components is practiced by reflecting on your actions, talking with others about how you’re doing, and making intentional choices to support your team. For example, you might delegate a task to a teammate and trust them to do it their way, or you might encourage someone who’s struggling by reminding them that mistakes are part of learning.

At different levels, Ownership looks a little different. At the first level, you might not even realize what Ownership is or what it involves. At the second level, you start to notice the value of reflecting on your actions and are willing to try new behaviors, like letting others lead or being more open to feedback. You’re making an effort to change your habits and take more responsibility. At the third level, Ownership becomes second nature—you naturally trust your team, set clear expectations, and encourage others without thinking about it. You set an example for others, help create a positive team culture, and make sure everyone feels empowered to contribute. In short, practicing Ownership means working together, learning from each other, and building habits that make the whole team stronger over time.
column7:
Practices:
1. A practice requires working with other people
2. A practice never stops being built; you never become an expert in a practice, but the behaviors of a practice become more permanent over time
3. A practice is rooted in beliefs and behaviors
4. A practice can be measured through what other people believe about you
5. A practice cannot be concretely measured through deliverables
6. A practice is an intentional choice someone makes to do
7. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting to yourself
8. A practice can be measured through what you believe about yourself
9. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting with others
Skills:
1. A skill enables you to make a deliverable
2. A skill is measured through how many times you have applied it
3. A skill is rooted in  knowledge and theory
4. A skill applies to a specific activity
5. A skill is expected to be performed in specific job functions
6. A skill is something someone can become an expert in it with a lot of experience
7. A skill you can perform yourself
8. A skill can be practiced by applying it to work you deliver or duties you perform
9. A skill is required in specific jobs, people may not have a choice to perform the skill if the job requires them
10. A skill gets practiced by doing work and performing duties

What they both have in common: 
1. A practice and a skill can be lost if  not continued to be practiced
2. A practice and a skill require willingness to perform
column8: Activities that involve interacting with other people that you always work on.

column11: Delegation,Growth Mindset,Willingness,Letting Go of Control,Expectation Setting,Esteem,Humility,Acknowledgement,Commitment,Removing Ego,Honesty In Self,Encouragement,Participation,Patience,Respect,Transferring Power,Trust
column12: Delegation is a component of Ownership practice that involves entrusting tasks or responsibilities to others, allowing them to take initiative and make decisions. This component includes beliefs and behaviors like trusting others’ abilities, being willing to let go of control, and supporting team members as they take on new roles. Successful delegation requires clear communication, understanding team strengths, and providing guidance without micromanaging. Delegation helps people develop leadership skills and fosters a sense of shared responsibility within a group, which is essential for effective ownership. It is assessed and practiced through questions like “I ask others and let others take lead on things that I could own,” which encourages reflection on one’s willingness to empower others and share responsibilities., Growth Mindset is a component of Ownership and Service Leadership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like embracing challenges, learning from mistakes, and understanding that abilities can be developed through effort and perseverance. People with a growth mindset see failures as opportunities to learn and improve, rather than as setbacks. They use language like “I can’t do this...yet,” which reflects the belief that skills and understanding can grow over time. Growth Mindset helps someone succeed by encouraging resilience, adaptability, and a willingness to seek feedback and try new strategies. It is closely related to Ownership, as it requires taking responsibility for one’s learning, and to Service Leadership, as it supports helping others grow and improve. This component is assessed and practiced through questions that prompt reflection on learning from failure and recognizing the potential for future growth, such as focusing on lessons learned and trusting in the “power of yet”., Willingness is a component of Ownership practice. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like being open to letting others take responsibility, actively encouraging participation, and intentionally creating opportunities for others to contribute and lead. People who succeed in Willingness demonstrate trust in their peers, show flexibility, and are motivated to support shared goals rather than control every outcome. Willingness is closely tied to Ownership because it requires individuals to step back and allow others to take charge, which helps build a sense of shared responsibility and empowerment within a group. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like "I purposefully want to provide space for others to own things," which encourages reflection on whether someone is intentionally making room for others to take initiative and responsibility., Letting Go of Control is a component of Ownership and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like trusting others, accepting uncertainty, and allowing situations to unfold without always intervening or micromanaging. People who succeed in Letting Go of Control are able to recognize when their need to direct outcomes is unhelpful, and instead focus on influencing what they can while accepting what they cannot change. This approach helps build resilience, encourages collaboration, and fosters a sense of shared responsibility, which are key aspects of Ownership and Empowerment. Letting Go of Control is assessed and practiced through questions like "I prioritize letting events play out over trying to take control over outcomes," which encourages reflection on one’s ability to step back and allow others or circumstances to take their course., Expectation Setting is a component of Ownership and Decision-Making practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like clearly communicating goals, roles, and responsibilities so that everyone understands what is expected of them and can take responsibility for their part of the work. People who succeed in Expectation Setting are good at making sure everyone is on the same page, asking questions to clarify any confusion, and making sure that expectations are realistic and agreed upon by all involved. Expectation Setting is closely related to Ownership because when expectations are clear, people are more likely to take responsibility for their work, and it supports Decision-Making by providing a shared understanding that guides choices and actions. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like “I work to build a shared understanding of outcomes so that people can own work,” which checks whether someone is actively making expectations clear and encouraging others to take ownership. Reputable sources highlight that effective expectation setting leads to better teamwork, accountability, and project success., Esteem is a component of Ownership, Empowerment, and Continuous Growth practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like recognizing and valuing your own abilities, as well as appreciating the contributions of others, regardless of their skill level. People who succeed in Esteem are confident in their strengths, encourage others, and foster a positive team environment where everyone feels valued. Esteem is closely related to the associated practices because it helps individuals take responsibility for their growth (Ownership), feel empowered to contribute (Empowerment), and support ongoing improvement for themselves and others (Continuous Growth). Esteem is assessed and practiced through questions that ask individuals to reflect on how they view their own and others’ abilities as meaningful contributions to the team, and whether they believe everyone brings value, no matter their skill level., Humility is a component of Service Leadership and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like recognizing your own limitations, valuing others’ perspectives, and being open to learning from mistakes. People who succeed in humility are able to listen without judgment, accept feedback, and avoid acting with dominance or arrogance. Humility helps build trust and respect within teams, making it easier to serve others and take responsibility for actions. It is closely related to Service Leadership, where leaders put others’ needs first, and Ownership, where individuals acknowledge their role in outcomes. Humility is assessed and practiced through questions like “I do not judge myself or others, I operate without dominance or arrogance,” which encourage self-reflection and awareness of one’s attitude and behavior toward others., Acknowledgement is a component of Service Leadership, Ownership, and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like actively listening to others, recognizing the validity of their feelings or perspectives, and expressing understanding, even when you disagree or see things differently. Acknowledgement helps build trust, respect, and open communication, which are essential for effective teamwork and leadership. It is closely related to Service Leadership, where leaders serve others by valuing their input; Ownership, where individuals take responsibility for their interactions; and Empowerment, where people feel heard and respected, encouraging them to contribute more confidently. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I acknowledge the truth in others' words often, even when I think they could be wrong or have a different perspective," which measures how often someone recognizes and validates others' viewpoints, fostering a more inclusive and supportive environment., Commitment is a component of Ownership, Service Leadership, and Decision-Making practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like keeping promises, following through on responsibilities, and being honest about what you can and cannot do. People who succeed in Commitment are reliable, communicate clearly if they need to change an agreement, and only take on tasks they are sure they can complete. Commitment supports Ownership by encouraging accountability, strengthens Service Leadership by building trust with others, and improves Decision-Making by ensuring choices are made with integrity and follow-through. Commitment is assessed and practiced through questions that ask if you do what you said you would do by the agreed time, if you make new agreements when needed, and if you only agree to things you can truly commit to., Removing Ego is a component of Ownership, Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Psychological Safety practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like recognizing that you do not have all the answers, valuing the contributions of others regardless of their experience, and being open to learning from teammates. Removing Ego helps someone succeed by fostering humility, encouraging collaboration, and creating an environment where everyone feels comfortable sharing ideas. It is closely related to the associated practices because it supports taking responsibility (Ownership), serving and uplifting others (Service Leadership), enabling others to contribute (Empowerment), and making it safe for everyone to speak up (Psychological Safety). This component is assessed and practiced through questions that ask whether you prioritize the mindset that you do not have all the right answers in teamwork and remind yourself that others can produce great things, even when they don’t have experience., Honesty In Self is a component of Service Leadership and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like being truthful with yourself, recognizing your own biases, and admitting when you are wrong or have made mistakes. It means being willing to look at your thoughts, feelings, and actions without denial or self-deception, which helps you grow and make better decisions. Honesty In Self is important for Service Leadership because leaders who are honest with themselves can serve others more effectively, and it supports Ownership by encouraging personal responsibility and accountability. This component is assessed and practiced through the question, "I seek to discover ways I am lying to myself," which encourages self-reflection and the ongoing habit of examining your own honesty and integrity., Encouragement is a component of Ownership and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like offering positive reinforcement, expressing support, and motivating others, especially when they face setbacks or failures. Encouragement helps people feel valued and confident, which increases their willingness to take initiative and responsibility for their actions. It is closely related to Ownership, as it fosters a sense of personal investment, and to Empowerment, as it builds the confidence needed to try new things and persist through challenges. Encouragement is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I provide positive reinforcement to others, even when they have tried and failed," which measures how often someone supports and uplifts others in difficult moments., Participation is a component of Service Leadership and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like actively engaging in group discussions, contributing ideas, and making decisions rather than passively agreeing just to avoid conflict. People who succeed in Participation are willing to share their perspectives, listen to others, and respectfully challenge ideas when necessary, which helps teams make better decisions and fosters a sense of belonging and responsibility. Participation is closely tied to Service Leadership, where individuals serve the group by contributing their strengths, and to Ownership, where individuals take responsibility for their role and impact within the team. This component is assessed and practiced through questions that ask whether someone participates in discussions and decisions, avoids simply going along with the group to prevent conflict, and sees themselves as a valuable contributor to the team., Patience is a component of Continuous Growth, Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like the ability to pause before reacting, wait for the right moment or for others to catch up, and exercise self-control even in challenging situations. Patience helps someone succeed by allowing them to thoughtfully consider their actions, support others’ development, and persist through difficulties without frustration. It is closely related to the associated practices because continuous growth requires time and perseverance, service leadership involves understanding and supporting others’ needs, empowerment means giving space for others to learn, and ownership requires managing one’s emotions and responses. Patience is assessed and practiced through questions like “I have the ability to pause, wait for alignment, exercise self-control,” which encourage reflection on how well someone manages their impulses and supports group progress., Respect is a component of Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like valuing the perspectives and experiences of others, listening actively, and acknowledging the strengths and wisdom that others bring, even when there are disagreements. Respect helps someone succeed by fostering trust, collaboration, and open-mindedness, which are essential for effective leadership, empowering others, and taking responsibility for shared goals. It is closely related to these practices because service leaders must honor those they serve, empowerment requires recognizing others’ contributions, and ownership involves appreciating diverse viewpoints. Respect is assessed and practiced through questions like “I recognize the wisdom of others. I acknowledge the ways other people are right even when there's disagreement,” which encourage self-reflection on how well one values and learns from others’ insights., Transferring Power is a component of Service Leadership, Ownership, and Psychological Safety practices that involves the belief that everyone has the capacity to make meaningful decisions and the behavior of actively encouraging others to take responsibility and make choices for themselves. This component is rooted in trust, respect, and the understanding that empowering others leads to greater engagement, growth, and innovation. Succeeding in Transferring Power requires leaders to step back, provide support, and create an environment where individuals feel safe and confident to take initiative. It is closely related to Service Leadership, which emphasizes serving others by enabling their success; Ownership, which focuses on individuals taking responsibility for outcomes; and Psychological Safety, which ensures people feel comfortable expressing ideas and making decisions without fear of negative consequences. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I believe that everyone has the ability to make decisions and want them to decide rather than make the decisions myself," which helps gauge a person's willingness to empower others and foster a collaborative, inclusive environment., Trust is a component of Psychological Safety, Service Leadership, Empowerment, Continuous Growth, and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like relying on the integrity and abilities of oneself and others, being honest, keeping commitments, and showing vulnerability by admitting mistakes or asking for help. Trust helps people feel safe to share ideas, take risks, and collaborate, which strengthens team relationships and supports growth and accountability. It is closely linked to psychological safety, as trust allows people to express themselves without fear, and it is essential for leaders who serve and empower others, as well as for individuals who take ownership and strive for continuous improvement. Trust is assessed and practiced through questions like "I value trust in myself and trust in others to carry out work," which encourages reflection on personal and team trustworthiness and the willingness to depend on others.

column14:
1. You are not aware of what makes up the practice
2. You are not willing to practice it
3. You tried to practice it, but stopped
column15: When you are at this practice level for Ownership, it means you are just starting out and don’t really know what Ownership is or what it looks like in action. You might not be aware of the beliefs and behaviors that make up Ownership, like trusting others, letting go of control, or encouraging your teammates to take responsibility. At this stage, you haven’t tried to practice Ownership yet, or maybe you tried once but stopped. You might not feel ready or willing to share responsibility, delegate tasks, or help others grow. This is a normal starting point—everyone begins here before they learn what Ownership really means and how it can help a team work better together.
column16:
1. You believe it's valuable to practice the practice
2. You are willing to practice it
3. You have knowledge about the components of the practice
4. You've started practicing with others and reflecting
column17: When you are at this practice level for Ownership, it means you not only see the value in taking responsibility and empowering others, but you are also willing to put these beliefs into action. You understand the different parts that make up Ownership—like delegation, trust, humility, and encouragement—and you’ve started practicing these behaviors with other people, not just by yourself. You reflect on your actions and talk with others about how you’re doing, which helps you grow and improve. At this stage, you’re intentionally making choices to share responsibility, support your team, and create an environment where everyone feels they can contribute and take charge. You know that Ownership isn’t about being perfect or always in control; it’s about working together, learning from each other, and building habits that make everyone stronger over time.
column18:
1. You've seen growth in your own practice through reflection
2. You are starting to work on changing habits
column19: When you are at this practice level for Ownership, it means you are starting to notice how reflecting on your actions and choices helps you grow. You’re beginning to see that by thinking about what you’ve done and how you could do better, you can improve how you work and interact with others. At this stage, you’re also starting to change some of your habits—maybe you’re trying to be more open to feedback, or you’re working on letting others take the lead sometimes. You’re not just doing things the way you always have; you’re making a real effort to adjust your behavior so you can take more responsibility and help your team succeed. This is an important step because it shows you’re willing to learn, adapt, and share responsibility, which are all key parts of true ownership.
column20:
1. Your habits are starting to become automatic in your practice with others
2. You’re so practiced in it, that you are a role model to others in the practice
3. You can and do advocate for the practice when working with others
column21: When you are at this practice level for Ownership, it means that taking responsibility and showing initiative with others has become a natural part of how you work. Your habits—like trusting teammates, letting others lead, setting clear expectations, and encouraging everyone’s growth—happen almost automatically. You don’t just do these things for yourself; you set an example for others, showing them what true ownership looks like in a team. You’re comfortable sharing responsibility, supporting others to step up, and you speak up for the importance of ownership when working with your group. People see you as a role model, and you help create a culture where everyone feels empowered to take charge and contribute.

column3: Psychological Safety is a practice that means making sure everyone on a team feels safe to share their ideas, ask questions, and even make mistakes without being judged or punished. Imagine a team as a big circle where everyone is invited to speak up, no matter their role or experience. This practice is built on beliefs and behaviors like listening to others, trusting each other, and encouraging everyone to join in. It never really ends—you keep building it by reflecting on how you and others feel and act together. Psychological Safety is measured by how much people feel included, able to learn, contribute, and challenge ideas, and by how much you and others believe everyone’s voice matters. It’s not about what you deliver, but about how you work with others. For example, if you’re in a tech job like software development, Psychological Safety helps teams solve problems faster and come up with better ideas because everyone feels comfortable sharing. Skills like Project Management or Coding are different—they’re specific things you can get really good at by doing them over and over, and you can measure them by what you produce, like a finished app or a project plan. Both practices and skills need you to keep using them, or you might lose them, and both require you to want to do them. In any job, having Psychological Safety as a practice helps everyone do their best work together, while skills help you get specific tasks done.
column4: The practice of Psychological Safety is important to cross-functional teamwork in a lot of ways. Psychological Safety means that everyone on the team feels safe to speak up, share ideas, ask questions, and even make mistakes without being afraid of being judged or punished. This is especially important when people from different roles—like project managers, designers, developers, researchers, and business analysts—work together. When team members feel safe, they are more likely to contribute their unique perspectives, which leads to better problem-solving and more creative solutions. It also helps teams learn from mistakes, adapt quickly, and support each other, making the whole group stronger and more successful. In short, Psychological Safety helps everyone do their best work together, no matter what their job is.
column5:
Psychological Safety is similar to other practices because it’s all about how people work together, focusing on beliefs and behaviors that help everyone feel included, respected, and able to speak up. Like other practices, it’s not something you ever fully master—it’s built over time by reflecting on your actions and how others feel around you. Psychological Safety shares components with other practices, such as Active Listening, Removing Ego, Transferring Power, Pause, Vulnerability, Approach, and Trust, which are also found in Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Ownership. These components all help create a positive team environment, but each practice uses them in slightly different ways. For example, while Trust is important in both Psychological Safety and Empowerment, in Psychological Safety it’s about making sure people feel safe to share ideas without fear, while in Empowerment it’s more about giving people the confidence to take action. Unlike skills, which are about what you can do on your own and can be measured by what you produce, practices like Psychological Safety are about how you interact with others and are measured by how people feel and what they believe about you.

What makes Psychological Safety unique is that its main goal is to create a team environment where everyone feels safe to be themselves, ask questions, make mistakes, and challenge ideas without worrying about being embarrassed or punished. Its components—like Inclusive Safety, Learner Safety, Contributor Safety, and Challenger Safety—are all about making sure every person, no matter their background or experience, feels valued and heard. This focus on emotional safety and open communication sets Psychological Safety apart from other practices, which might focus more on leadership, decision-making, or getting things done. Psychological Safety is the foundation that allows teams to innovate, learn, and grow together, making it a key practice for any group that wants to do their best work.
column6:
This practice is carried out through a set of beliefs and behaviors that help everyone on a team feel safe, respected, and able to share their ideas without fear. Psychological Safety is built by including everyone, encouraging learning, welcoming all contributions, and supporting people who challenge the way things are done. It involves listening carefully, inviting quieter people to speak, showing trust, and being open about your own mistakes. The main components—like Inclusive Safety, Learner Safety, Contributor Safety, Challenger Safety, Active Listening, Removing Ego, Transferring Power, Pause, Vulnerability, Approach, and Trust—are all about making sure everyone feels valued and empowered to participate. For example, you might ask everyone for their opinions in a meeting, admit when you don’t know something, or encourage someone to try a new idea even if it might not work out. These actions help create a team where people feel comfortable being themselves and working together.

There are different levels to practicing Psychological Safety. At Level 1, you’re just starting out: you know it’s important and you’re willing to try, but you’re still learning what it really means and how to do it. At Level 2, you’re reflecting on your actions and making changes—like pausing to let others speak or asking for feedback—so you can make everyone feel more included and safe. At Level 3, these behaviors become second nature; you automatically help others feel welcome, respected, and able to share their thoughts, making your team a place where everyone can do their best work.
column7:
Practices:
1. A practice requires working with other people
2. A practice never stops being built; you never become an expert in a practice, but the behaviors of a practice become more permanent over time
3. A practice is rooted in beliefs and behaviors
4. A practice can be measured through what other people believe about you
5. A practice cannot be concretely measured through deliverables
6. A practice is an intentional choice someone makes to do
7. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting to yourself
8. A practice can be measured through what you believe about yourself
9. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting with others
Skills:
1. A skill enables you to make a deliverable
2. A skill is measured through how many times you have applied it
3. A skill is rooted in  knowledge and theory
4. A skill applies to a specific activity
5. A skill is expected to be performed in specific job functions
6. A skill is something someone can become an expert in it with a lot of experience
7. A skill you can perform yourself
8. A skill can be practiced by applying it to work you deliver or duties you perform
9. A skill is required in specific jobs, people may not have a choice to perform the skill if the job requires them
10. A skill gets practiced by doing work and performing duties

What they both have in common: 
1. A practice and a skill can be lost if  not continued to be practiced
2. A practice and a skill require willingness to perform

column12: Inclusive Safety is a component of Psychological Safety practice that involves creating an environment where everyone, regardless of their role, identity, background, experience, or status, feels respected, valued, and empowered to contribute their ideas and perspectives. This component includes beliefs and behaviors like actively listening to all team members, encouraging participation from everyone, and recognizing the unique strengths each person brings to the group. Inclusive Safety helps people succeed by fostering trust and openness, which leads to better collaboration and innovation. It is closely related to Psychological Safety because both focus on making team members feel safe to express themselves without fear of rejection or embarrassment. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I believe that everyone on the team, even the interns, have valuable perspective and decision-making abilities," which encourages reflection on whether all voices are truly welcomed and valued in team discussions., Learner Safety is a component of Psychological Safety practice that involves creating an environment where individuals feel comfortable taking risks, asking questions, and making mistakes as part of the learning process. This component includes beliefs and behaviors like encouraging curiosity, supporting experimentation, and viewing failure as a valuable opportunity for growth rather than something to be punished or avoided. When Learner Safety is present, people are more likely to engage actively, share ideas, and persist through challenges, which helps them develop new skills and knowledge. The assessment question, "I believe that everyone should learn by doing and failing," evaluates whether individuals and groups embrace the idea that learning often involves trial and error, and whether they support each other through setbacks. Practicing Learner Safety means fostering a culture where mistakes are seen as essential steps toward improvement, which is fundamental to Psychological Safety practice., Contributor Safety is a component of Psychological Safety practice that involves creating an environment where all team members feel their ideas and input are welcomed and valued, regardless of their experience or background. This component includes beliefs and behaviors like encouraging participation from everyone, listening actively, and showing respect for diverse perspectives. People succeed in Contributor Safety by being open to new ideas, supporting others when they share, and avoiding dismissive or judgmental reactions. Contributor Safety is closely tied to Psychological Safety because when individuals feel safe to contribute, teams benefit from a wider range of ideas and solutions. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I believe that everyone has something valuable to contribute on teams, even if they have little to no experience," which helps gauge whether team members feel empowered to share and whether the team values contributions from all members., Challenger Safety is a component of Psychological Safety practice that involves creating an environment where individuals feel safe and empowered to question the status quo, challenge existing processes, and propose new ideas without fear of negative consequences. This component includes beliefs and behaviors like encouraging open dialogue, respecting diverse viewpoints, and valuing constructive dissent as a way to drive innovation and improvement. People succeed in Challenger Safety by fostering trust, demonstrating respect for others’ input, and being willing to listen and adapt. It is closely related to Psychological Safety because both focus on building a culture where everyone feels comfortable speaking up and contributing. Challenger Safety is assessed and practiced through questions that gauge whether individuals believe in the value of challenging current ways of working or decisions to find better solutions, such as the assessment question provided. Reputable sources like Harvard Business Review and research by Dr. Timothy R. Clark emphasize that organizations with high Challenger Safety are more adaptive and innovative, as employees are not afraid to voice concerns or suggest changes., Active Listening is a component of Service Leadership, Decision-Making, Psychological Safety, Continuous Growth, and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like giving full attention to the speaker, withholding judgment, reflecting back what has been said, and acknowledging others’ perspectives before responding. Active Listening helps someone succeed by building trust, clarifying understanding, and encouraging open communication, which are essential for effective leadership, sound decision-making, creating safe environments, ongoing personal development, and empowering others. It is assessed and practiced through questions like “I repeat back what people have shared and acknowledge their views before responding,” which checks for the ability to listen attentively, confirm understanding, and show respect for others’ input., Removing Ego is a component of Ownership, Service Leadership, Empowerment, and Psychological Safety practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like recognizing that you do not have all the answers, valuing the contributions of others regardless of their experience, and being open to learning from teammates. Removing Ego helps someone succeed by fostering humility, encouraging collaboration, and creating an environment where everyone feels comfortable sharing ideas. It is closely related to the associated practices because it supports taking responsibility (Ownership), serving and uplifting others (Service Leadership), enabling others to contribute (Empowerment), and making it safe for everyone to speak up (Psychological Safety). This component is assessed and practiced through questions that ask whether you prioritize the mindset that you do not have all the right answers in teamwork and remind yourself that others can produce great things, even when they don’t have experience., Transferring Power is a component of Service Leadership, Ownership, and Psychological Safety practices that involves the belief that everyone has the capacity to make meaningful decisions and the behavior of actively encouraging others to take responsibility and make choices for themselves. This component is rooted in trust, respect, and the understanding that empowering others leads to greater engagement, growth, and innovation. Succeeding in Transferring Power requires leaders to step back, provide support, and create an environment where individuals feel safe and confident to take initiative. It is closely related to Service Leadership, which emphasizes serving others by enabling their success; Ownership, which focuses on individuals taking responsibility for outcomes; and Psychological Safety, which ensures people feel comfortable expressing ideas and making decisions without fear of negative consequences. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, "I believe that everyone has the ability to make decisions and want them to decide rather than make the decisions myself," which helps gauge a person's willingness to empower others and foster a collaborative, inclusive environment., Pause is a component of Empowerment, Psychological Safety, Decision-Making, and Service Leadership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like intentionally creating space in conversations for reflection, allowing others to process information, and encouraging everyone to contribute their thoughts without feeling rushed. Pausing helps people feel valued and heard, which builds trust and confidence in group settings. It is closely related to the associated practices because it empowers individuals to speak up, fosters a safe environment for sharing ideas, supports thoughtful decision-making, and demonstrates a service-oriented approach to leadership. The component is assessed and practiced through questions like "I actively prioritize pausing in conversations to ensure that people in the room are able to provide input and voice," which measures how well someone uses pauses to include others and promote open dialogue., Vulnerability is a component of Service Leadership and Psychological Safety practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like openly sharing your true feelings, admitting mistakes, and expressing fears or struggles with others to foster trust and connection within a team. People who succeed in vulnerability are honest about their limitations and emotions, which helps create an environment where others feel safe to do the same. Vulnerability is essential for building strong relationships, encouraging open communication, and supporting team members’ confidence and growth. It is closely related to Service Leadership, where leaders serve others by being authentic and approachable, and to Psychological Safety, where team members feel secure enough to take risks and be themselves. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, “I prioritize sharing my true feelings and my mistakes, fears, and struggles to others for the benefit of building teammate confidence,” which measures how willing someone is to be open for the good of the team., Approach is a component of Psychological Safety, Service Leadership, and Empowerment practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like actively seeking to understand others’ preferences and needs, rather than assuming your own way of support is best. People who succeed in Approach listen carefully, ask open-ended questions, and adapt their actions based on feedback, which helps build trust and respect within a group. Approach is closely related to Psychological Safety because it encourages open communication and reduces fear of judgment, to Service Leadership because it centers on serving others’ needs, and to Empowerment because it gives people a voice in how they are supported. This component is assessed and practiced through questions like, “I ask others how they want to be served and supported instead of supporting them in the way I want to be supported,” which measures whether someone is prioritizing others’ perspectives and adapting their approach accordingly., Trust is a component of Psychological Safety, Service Leadership, Empowerment, Continuous Growth, and Ownership practices. This component involves beliefs and behaviors like relying on the integrity and abilities of oneself and others, being honest, keeping commitments, and showing vulnerability by admitting mistakes or asking for help. Trust helps people feel safe to share ideas, take risks, and collaborate, which strengthens team relationships and supports growth and accountability. It is closely linked to psychological safety, as trust allows people to express themselves without fear, and it is essential for leaders who serve and empower others, as well as for individuals who take ownership and strive for continuous improvement. Trust is assessed and practiced through questions like "I value trust in myself and trust in others to carry out work," which encourages reflection on personal and team trustworthiness and the willingness to depend on others.

column15: When you are at this practice level for Psychological Safety, it means you are not yet aware of what Psychological Safety really is or what it looks like in action. You might not know that it’s about making sure everyone on a team feels safe to speak up, share ideas, ask questions, or admit mistakes without being afraid of being judged, embarrassed, or punished. At this stage, you haven’t started to notice or think about the beliefs and behaviors—like listening to others, encouraging participation, or showing trust—that help create a safe and open environment for everyone. In simple terms, you’re not yet paying attention to whether people feel comfortable and respected in group settings, and you haven’t begun to reflect on how your actions or beliefs might affect the safety and confidence of others on your team.
column16:
1. You believe it's valuable to practice the practice
2. You are willing to practice it
3. You have knowledge about the components of the practice
4. You've started practicing with others and reflecting
column17: When you are at this practice level for Psychological Safety, it means you’re just starting to build the foundation for making your team a safe and open place. At this stage, you recognize that Psychological Safety is important—you see the value in making sure everyone feels comfortable sharing ideas, asking questions, and even making mistakes without fear of embarrassment or punishment. You’re willing to try out these behaviors, and you’ve learned about the key parts of Psychological Safety, like making sure everyone feels included, encouraging learning, welcoming contributions from all team members, and supporting people who challenge the status quo. You’ve begun to practice these ideas with others, maybe by listening more carefully, inviting quieter teammates to speak up, or reflecting on how your actions affect the group. You’re also starting to think about how your team feels and how you can help everyone feel respected and valued. This is the first step in a journey—by practicing and reflecting with others, you’ll keep getting better at creating a safe and supportive team environment.
column18:
1. You've seen growth in your own practice through reflection
2. You are starting to work on changing habits
column19: When you are at this practice level for Psychological Safety, it means you’ve started to notice positive changes in how you interact with others by thinking about your own actions and attitudes. You’re reflecting on your behavior—like whether you listen to everyone, encourage questions, or make it safe for people to share ideas—and you’re beginning to change old habits that might have made others feel left out or afraid to speak up. For example, you might catch yourself interrupting someone and then make an effort to pause and let them finish, or you might start asking quieter teammates for their opinions. At this stage, you’re not just learning about psychological safety—you’re actively working to make it a regular part of how you work with others, even if it still feels new or takes effort. This is an important step because it shows you’re committed to creating a team environment where everyone feels respected, valued, and safe to contribute.
column20:
1. Your habits are starting to become automatic in your practice with others
2. You’re so practiced in it, that you are a role model to others in the practice
3. You can and do advocate for the practice when working with others
column21: When you are at this practice level for Psychological Safety, it means that your habits for making others feel safe, respected, and included are starting to become automatic when you work with people. You don’t have to remind yourself to listen to everyone, encourage questions, or make space for different ideas—it’s just how you naturally act in group settings. For example, if someone new joins your team, you automatically make sure they feel welcome and comfortable sharing their thoughts, even if they’re nervous or unsure. You’re building a strong foundation where everyone feels they can speak up, ask questions, and even make mistakes without fear of being judged or ignored. This level is about consistently showing these behaviors so that others can rely on you to help create a safe and open environment every time you work together.

column3: Service Leadership is a practice where you intentionally choose to work with others by focusing on serving their needs, building trust, and supporting their growth, rather than just leading from the front. It’s like being the roots of a tree, quietly supporting everyone above ground so they can grow strong. Service Leadership is built on beliefs and behaviors like honesty, empathy, humility, and active listening, and you never really “finish” learning it—you get better by reflecting on your actions, listening to feedback from others, and noticing how you make people feel. Unlike a skill, which is something you can do by yourself and get better at by repeating it (like project management or coding), a practice like Service Leadership is measured by how others experience you and how you see yourself growing over time. Both practices and skills need you to keep working at them, or you’ll lose them, and both require you to want to improve. In any tech job, Service Leadership helps teams work better together, solve problems with care, and create a positive environment where everyone can do their best work, making it just as important as technical skills for long-term success.
column4: The practice of Service Leadership is important to cross-functional teamwork in a lot of ways. Service Leadership means putting the needs of others first, building trust, and helping everyone do their best work. In teams where people have different roles—like project managers, designers, developers, analysts, and more—Service Leadership helps everyone feel valued and supported. When team members act with honesty, empathy, humility, and respect, it creates a safe space where people can share ideas, ask questions, and learn from each other. This leads to better problem-solving, stronger relationships, and more creative solutions. Service Leadership also means being reliable, listening actively, and encouraging others to take part and grow, which helps the whole team succeed together. By practicing Service Leadership, everyone in the team—no matter their job—can help create a positive, productive environment where everyone can thrive.
column5:
Service Leadership is similar to other practices because, like them, it’s all about how you interact with and support other people, and it’s something you keep working on throughout your life—you never really “finish” getting better at it. Practices, including Service Leadership, are based on beliefs and behaviors, like being honest, caring, and listening to others, and they’re measured by how you and others see your actions, not by what you produce. This is different from skills, which are about what you can do or make, like managing a project or designing a website, and are measured by how often you do them and how well you perform. Service Leadership shares some components with other practices, such as Honesty, Humility, Commitment, and Active Listening, which are also found in Ownership, Empowerment, and Psychological Safety. However, the way these components show up in Service Leadership is focused on serving and uplifting others, making sure everyone feels valued, and putting the needs of the group before your own ego.

What makes Service Leadership unique is that it’s all about leading by serving others—putting their needs first, building trust, and creating a positive environment where everyone can grow and succeed together. While other practices might focus on personal responsibility (Ownership) or making sure everyone feels safe to speak up (Psychological Safety), Service Leadership is special because it combines qualities like empathy, kindness, care, and transferring power to others, so that leadership isn’t about being in charge, but about helping everyone do their best. This means Service Leadership is less about what you achieve on your own and more about how you help others shine, making it a practice that’s all about teamwork, support, and making a difference for the whole group.
column6:
This practice is carried out through a set of beliefs and behaviors that focus on serving and supporting others, rather than just leading from the front. Service Leadership means being honest with yourself and others, listening carefully, showing empathy, and helping your team grow. It’s about putting the needs of your team first, building trust, and creating an environment where everyone feels valued and heard. You practice Service Leadership by reflecting on your actions, asking for feedback, and making intentional choices to act with kindness, respect, and humility. It’s not about being perfect, but about making a real effort every day to support and uplift those around you.

At different levels, Service Leadership looks a little different. At the first level, you’re just starting out—you know why Service Leadership matters and you’re learning what it looks like. You try to use these behaviors when you work with others and reflect on how you can improve. At the second level, you’re more aware of your habits and how they affect your team. You notice when you make mistakes, like being impatient or not listening, and you work to do better next time. You’re honest with yourself and open to learning, even when it’s hard. At the third level, Service Leadership becomes second nature. You act with honesty, empathy, and care without having to think about it, and others see you as a role model. You encourage your team to practice Service Leadership too, helping to create a positive and supportive culture for everyone.
column7:
Practices:
1. A practice requires working with other people
2. A practice never stops being built; you never become an expert in a practice, but the behaviors of a practice become more permanent over time
3. A practice is rooted in beliefs and behaviors
4. A practice can be measured through what other people believe about you
5. A practice cannot be concretely measured through deliverables
6. A practice is an intentional choice someone makes to do
7. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting to yourself
8. A practice can be measured through what you believe about yourself
9. You learn how to get good about your practice by reflecting with others
Skills:
1. A skill enables you to make a deliverable
2. A skill is measured through how many times you have applied it
3. A skill is rooted in  knowledge and theory
4. A skill applies to a specific activity
5. A skill is expected to be performed in specific job functions
6. A skill is something someone can become an expert in it with a lot of experience
7. A skill you can perform yourself
8. A skill can be practiced by applying it to work you deliver or duties you perform
9. A skill is required in specific jobs, people may not have a choice to perform the skill if the job requires them
10. A skill gets practiced by doing work and performing duties

What they both have in common: 
1. A practice and a skill can be lost if  not continued to be practiced
2. A practice and a skill require willingness to perform
column8: Activities that involve interacting with other people that you always work on.
